vBRIEFInfo.description: Scope vBRIEF ingested from GitHub issue #1488

plan.title: RFC: demote the 1000-line file cap from MUST to SHOULD (unenforced, self-violated, anti-deep-modules)

narratives.Description: RFC: demote the 1000-line file cap from MUST to SHOULD (unenforced, self-violated, anti-deep-modules)
narratives.Overview:
## Summary

The 1000-line file cap is currently encoded as an RFC2119 `MUST` (`coding/coding.md:43`, `coding/coding.md:202`), but it is **unenforced**, **routinely violated by our own production code**, and **in tension with the framework's own "deep modules" architecture goal**. An unenforced, widely-broken `MUST` erodes the authority of every other `MUST` in the framework.

This issue proposes demoting the hard per-file line cap to a `SHOULD`/review-trigger and keeping cohesion (one responsibility per file) as the real hard rule.

## Where the rule lives

```
- ~ Files <300 lines ideal; files <500 lines recommended; ! files <1000 lines maximum   (coding/coding.md:43)
- ⊗ Files over 1000 lines                                                                (coding/coding.md:202)
```

Note: `pyproject.toml:78-80` attributes the cap to "deft/main.md", but `main.md` does not actually state it — the rule lives only in `coding/coding.md`. `coding/hygiene.md` (the ongoing-codebase-health doc) never mentions file size at all.

## Evidence

### 1. We don't obey our own MUST (dogfooding failure)

24 Python files exceed 1000 lines; **12 are production source** under `scripts/`:

- `scripts/migrate_vbrief.py` — 2,589 (2.6x the cap)
- `scripts/doctor.py` — 2,217
- `scripts/release.py` — 2,027
- `scripts/triage_help.py` — 1,684
- `scripts/triage_queue.py` — 1,139
- `scripts/triage_bootstrap.py` — 1,085
- `scripts/vbrief_validate.py` — 1,083
- `scripts/reconcile_issues.py` — 1,063
- `scripts/relocate.py` — 1,030
- `scripts/cache.py` — 1,021
- `scripts/issue_ingest.py` — 1,017
- `scripts/triage_summary.py` — 1,011

(The remaining 12 are test files, e.g. `tests/cli/test_migrate_vbrief.py` at 2,954.)

`pyproject.toml:76-80` even documents a workaround (`combine-as-imports = true`) introduced specifically to keep `migrate_vbrief.py` "under the 1000-line MUST limit." That file is now 2.6x over — the workaround failed and the rule was never enforced.

### 2. It is enforced nowhere

- `ruff` config (`pyproject.toml:72-73`) selects no module-length rule (no `PLR0915`, no pylint `C0302`).
- `scripts/doctor.py` has no line-count check.
- The `deft-directive-pre-pr` skill has no file-size check.
- No `task` target checks it.

The cap relies entirely on agent goodwill.

### 3. It violates our own Rule Authority axiom

`main.md:50-58` (Rule Authority [AXIOM]): *"Every rule MUST use the strongest applicable layer. Order: deterministic > Taskfile > vBRIEF > RFC2119 > prose."* A line count is trivially deterministic, so encoding it as RFC2119 prose is itself an axiom violation. Either enforce it deterministically or stop calling it a `MUST`.

### 4. It is in tension with our "deep modules" goal

The `deft-directive-gh-arch` skill is explicitly about "deepen shallow modules." A hard per-file line cap pressures contributors toward the shallow-module fragmentation (Ousterhout's "classitis") that this skill — and good architecture — warns against. Line count is a weak proxy; the real rule is one line above at `coding/coding.md:42`: `! One responsibility per file/module`.

## Industry context

- Linters that cap file length ship the rule as a **warning with per-file opt-outs**, not a build-breaking error (pylint `max-module-lines` default 1000 — likely our origin; ESLint `max-lines` default 300; Checkstyle `FileLength` default 2000; SonarQube treats it as a "code smell").
- Empirical evidence linking file size to defects is weak and confounded by total code volume; LOC is a poor quality metric and no study establishes 1000 as meaningful.
- Mature teams cap **function length / cyclomatic complexity** far more often than whole files, and optimize for **cohesion**, not raw line count.

The mainstream position: a 1000-line `MUST` is stricter than the tools that inspired it, unsupported by evidence, and architecturally counterproductive.

## Recommendations

### Option A (recommended) — demote to a SHOULD heuristic

- `coding/coding.md:43` → `~ files <300 ideal, <500 recommended, <1000 a review trigger — split when exceeded unless the file is genuinely single-responsibility`
- `coding/coding.md:202` → reframe the anti-pattern from "Files over 1000 lines" to "Large multi-responsibility files (size is a trigger to check cohesion, not an automatic defect)"
- Keeps the load-bearing `MUST` ("one responsibility per file"); makes size advisory.
- Zero code changes required; the codebase becomes immediately honest about the rule.
- Update the stale `pyproject.toml:76-80` comment (drop the "MUST limit" / "deft/main.md" references).

### Option B — keep the MUST but make it real (per the axiom)

- Add a deterministic line-count check wired into `task check` (and/or `doctor.py` / pre-pr).
- Bring the 12 production offenders into compliance by splitting, OR record documented exemptions (generated/aggregator files) with justification.
- More work, and arguably overkill given cohesion — not line count — is the actual goal.

## Acceptance criteria

- A decision is recorded (Option A, Option B, or a documented alternative).
- If Option A: `coding/coding.md:43`, `coding/coding.md:202`, and the `pyproject.toml` comment are updated; content tests referencing the cap (if any) are updated.
- If Option B: a deterministic check exists and passes, and either all offenders are split or each has a recorded exemption.

## References

- `coding/coding.md:42-43`, `coding/coding.md:202`
- `coding/hygiene.md` (no file-size rule)
- `main.md:50-58` (Rule Authority axiom)
- `pyproject.toml:72-80`
- `deft-directive-gh-arch` skill (deep modules)
